🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[🔧 STILL IN PROGRESS\]

*Name:* Glanworth Castle
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* kingdom of munster
*County:* County Cork
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 13th century
*Coords:* 52.187, -8.356
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glanworth
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Glanworth%20Castle/@52.187,-8.371,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/glanworth-castle.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* no
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* more research 

*Description:*
Glanworth is a village on the R512 regional road in County Cork, Ireland. It lies approximately 8 kilometres (5 mi) northwest of the town of Fermoy and 40 km (25 mi) northeast of Cork city. As of 2022, Glanworth's population was 628.

_Listing 1 of 238_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[🔧 STILL IN PROGRESS\]

*Name:* Harry Avery Castle
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* ulster and the north
*County:* County Tyrone
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 14th century
*Coords:* 54.713, -7.393
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harry_Avery%27s_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Harry%20Avery%20Castle/@54.64,-7.383,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/wiki-harry-avery-castle.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* more research 

*Description:*
Harry Avery's Castle is situated half a mile south-west of Newtownstewart, in County Tyrone, Northern Ireland. It is a rare example of a stone castle built by a Gaelic Irish chief, although its origins and history are uncertain. It is associated with and named after Henry Aimhréidh O'Neill, whose name was anglicised as Harry Avery.

_Listing 2 of 238_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[🔧 STILL IN PROGRESS\]

*Name:* Drumlane Abbey
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* ulster and the north
*County:* County Cavan
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* Early Medieval-15th century
*Coords:* 54.05722222, -7.47805556
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drumlane_abbey
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Drumlane%20Abbey/@54.071,-7.523,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZMEy1dIJ53Hk5IlYGtOIwFROzyQV-BrVjsC7sOVSlBQl6Cexs7b5uu75qtQLB13-luOHgJZbG08WLFDzPQUZc2Sd2QeLPkZJgN4trhJc_FHIwcPtgUZc3ZI-q7ww0OPGv3ZK2vJJvc8BgoFLDP5n9S4-A=s4800-w800
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* more research 

*Description:*
Drumlane is a townland near the village of Milltown in County Cavan, Ireland. The area of Drumlane is approximately 85.76 hectares. Drumlane is also the name of the civil parish in which the townland is situated. Christianity was introduced to Drumlane in 555 by Saint Columba, and Saint Máedóc of Ferns, known locally as Saint Mogue, founded an early Drumlane monastery. Saint Mogue made the Connachta nobleman Faircheallaigh the first Abbot of Drumlane at the end of the sixth century, and his Ó Faircheallaigh descendants were historically the Abbots of Drumlane. The name Drumlane denotes the drumlin region of low hilly ribbed moraines formed over a limestone bedrock created by the movement of glacial ice and melt water during the last ice age. Several townlands in the neighbourhood are prefixed with the word 'Drum' (Droim), while several others are prefixed with the word 'Derry' (Doire), which is Irish for oak.

_Listing 3 of 238_
